html:
[3037]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 以文笔依荆州成汭 - To rely on his literary skills in Jingzhou under Cheng Rui.
  • 自比陈阮 - To compare himself to Chen and Ruan.
  • 集其所作为十卷号刘表军书 - To compile his works into ten volumes titled 'Military Records of Liu Biao'.
  • 辞体不雅 - The style is not elegant.
  • 祝朝贵书 - A letter to congratulate the noble official.
  • 中书舍人草麻通事舍人曰奏可 - The Palace Secretary drafts the document, and the Attendant Secretary says it can be presented.
  • 贺襄州赵洪嗣袭书 - A letter to congratulate Zhao Hong on inheriting his position in Xiangzhou.
  • 不沐浴佩玉而石祁兆不登山取符而无恤封 - Without bathing and wearing jade, he does not ascend to the mountain to take the talisman, nor does he show concern for the seal.
  • 应举日诗卷题水牛 - On the day of the imperial examination, the poetry scroll titled 'Water Buffalo'.
  • 防犊横身立逢人揭尾跳 - The calf stands sideways, and when it meets people, it lifts its tail and jumps.
  • 朝士以为大笑 - The court officials found it amusing.
  • 一子忘其名 - One son whose name is forgotten.
  • 少年闻说壁鱼入道经函中 - As a young man, he heard that a bookworm entered the Daoist scripture.
  • 蠧蚀神仙字身有五色 - The bookworm ate the divine words, and the bookworm had five colors.
  • 人能取壁鱼吞之以致神仙而上升 - If a person could take the bookworm and swallow it, they could become a divine being and ascend to heaven.
  • 张子感之乃书神仙字碎剪寘于瓶中 - Zhang's son was moved by this and wrote the divine words, cut them into pieces, and placed them in a bottle.
  • 捉壁鱼以投之 - He caught the bookworm and threw it into the bottle.
  • 兾其蠧蚀亦欲吞之 - He hoped the bookworm would eat the divine words and he would swallow it.
  • 遂成心疾 - He then developed a mental illness.
  • 每一发竟日不食言词麤都无所避 - Each time he had an episode, he would go an entire day without eating, and his words were coarse and uncensored.
  • 其家扃闭而守之 - His family locked him up and guarded him.
  • 其愈既如常而倍食一月食料须品味而饫之 - They waited for him to recover, and once he did, he ate twice as much food, requiring a variety of dishes.
  • 嵗久方卒 - After many years, he finally died.
  • 是知心灵物也一伤神气善犹不可况为恶乎 - This shows that the spirit is a delicate thing; even minor damage to one's spirit is harmful, how much more so for major harm?
  • 即刘辟吞人张子吞神仙善恶不同其伤一也 - Just as Liu Bi swallowed people and Zhang's son swallowed divine beings, the harm is the same whether it is good or bad.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 荆州 - A region in present-day Hubei and Hunan provinces, historically significant during the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 成汭 - A historical figure, likely a local official or military leader in Jingzhou.
  • 陈阮 - Refers to Chen Lin and Ruan Yu, famous literary figures from the Han dynasty.
  • 刘表 - A warlord who controlled Jingzhou during the late Eastern Han period.
  • 襄州 - A region in present-day Hubei province.
  • 赵洪 - Likely a historical figure, possibly a noble or official in Xiangzhou.
  • 张裼 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official or noble.
  • 壁鱼 - A bookworm, often used in Chinese literature to symbolize the destruction of knowledge.
  • 道经 - Daoist scriptures, religious texts associated with Daoism.
  • 刘辟 - A historical figure, possibly a warlord or official who committed atrocities.
  • 神仙 - Divine beings or immortals in Chinese mythology.

Proper Nouns

  • 荆州 - A region in present-day Hubei and Hunan provinces.
  • 成汭 - A historical figure.
  • 陈阮 - Famous literary figures from the Han dynasty.
  • 刘表 - A warlord who controlled Jingzhou.
  • 襄州 - A region in present-day Hubei province.
  • 赵洪 - A historical figure.
  • 张裼 - A historical figure.
  • 壁鱼 - A bookworm.
  • 道经 - Daoist scriptures.
  • 刘辟 - A historical figure.
  • 神仙 - Divine beings or immortals.

[3038]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 破的 - Term indicating hitting the target or achieving the goal.
  • 悬之在璧 - Metaphorical expression possibly referring to something being displayed prominently.
  • 献赋之来下视同辈 - Phrase indicating that someone looks down on their peers when presenting a poem.
  • 仆马鲜华 - Description of someone's appearance or belongings.
  • 赍缗三千 - Phrase indicating carrying a large sum of money.
  • 江南所出 - Reference to goods or items from the southern regions.
  • 卜举人连中成败 - Phrase indicating predicting the success or failure of candidates in an examination.
  • 卦一缣 - Phrase indicating receiving a reward for a successful prediction.
  • 意在必取 - Phrase indicating having a strong desire to achieve something.
  • 短于呈试家 - Phrase indicating being weak in presenting oneself in a competitive setting.
  • 颇有生涯 - Phrase indicating having a somewhat prosperous life.

Proper Nouns

  • 刘义方 - Proper noun referring to Liu Yifang, a person's name.
  • 莫后反 - Phonetic transcription of a word or term.
  • 秀才郑羣玉 - Proper noun referring to Zheng Qunyu, a person's name.

[3039]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 入试不持书防人 - Colloquial phrase meaning 'entered the examination without carrying any books or defenses,' indicating a lack of preparation.
  • 油然易直子谅之心 - Poetic phrase meaning 'gently and straightforwardly with a heart of trust and understanding,' describing a calm and honest state of mind.
  • 押字须商量 - Colloquial phrase meaning 'the placement of characters needs to be discussed,' emphasizing the importance of careful consideration in composition.
  • 争应进士举 - Phrase meaning 'struggle to respond to the imperial examination,' indicating the competitive nature of the exam.
  • 犬蹲其傍 - Vivid phrase meaning 'a dog crouching by the side,' used to describe a physical scene.
  • 鸱拂其上 - Vivid phrase meaning 'a kite brushing against the top,' used to describe another physical scene.
  • 青玉案 - Poetic term referring to a jade tray, often used in classical literature to symbolize elegance and refinement.
  • 无名子 - Term meaning 'anonymous' or 'nameless,' often used to describe works or individuals without a known identity.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 梅权衡 - Historical figure, likely a scholar or poet from the Tang dynasty.
  • 张季遐 - Historical figure, likely a contemporary or rival of梅权衡.
  • 罗虬 - Historical figure, likely a poet or scholar from the Tang dynasty.
  • 皇甫松 - Historical figure, likely a poet or scholar from the Tang dynasty.
  • 齐防凌纂要 - Work or collection of writings, likely a compilation of important texts or ideas.
  • 吴王 - Historical figure, likely a ruler or noble from the Tang dynasty.
  • 天帝纪 - Historical or mythological text, likely a chronicle or record of divine events.
  • 卢庸 - Historical figure, likely a scholar or poet from the Tang dynasty.
  • 文宣王庙 - Temple dedicated to Confucius, a central figure in Chinese culture and philosophy.
  • 咸通 - Reign title of Emperor Xianzong of Tang, indicating the time period of the events described.

Proper Nouns

  • 梅权衡 - Historical figure, likely a scholar or poet from the Tang dynasty.
  • 张季遐 - Historical figure, likely a contemporary or rival of梅权衡.
  • 罗虬 - Historical figure, likely a poet or scholar from the Tang dynasty.
  • 皇甫松 - Historical figure, likely a poet or scholar from the Tang dynasty.
  • 齐防凌纂要 - Work or collection of writings, likely a compilation of important texts or ideas.
  • 吴王 - Historical figure, likely a ruler or noble from the Tang dynasty.
  • 天帝纪 - Historical or mythological text, likely a chronicle or record of divine events.
  • 卢庸 - Historical figure, likely a scholar or poet from the Tang dynasty.
  • 文宣王庙 - Temple dedicated to Confucius, a central figure in Chinese culture and philosophy.
  • 咸通 - Reign title of Emperor Xianzong of Tang, indicating the time period of the events described.

[3040]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 太平广记 - A famous Chinese collection of stories and anecdotes from various historical periods, often containing supernatural and legendary elements.
  • 嗤鄙五 - A section title that likely refers to stories or anecdotes involving ridicule, contempt, or foolishness.
  • 唐【阙】 - The word '唐' (Tang) suggests a reference to the Tang Dynasty, but the text is incomplete ('【阙】' indicates a missing part).
  • 前进士 - A title indicating a candidate who has passed the imperial examination but has not yet been officially appointed as an official.
  • 中原乱离 - Refers to a period of chaos and disorder in the Central Plains, likely during the late Tang or early Song dynasties.
  • 客于边上 - Means 'to reside on the frontier' or 'to live in a border region.'
  • 刺郡者亦是朝僚 - Means 'those who submit petitions to the provincial governor are also officials of the court.'
  • 多勉而【阙】 - The text is incomplete, but '多勉' suggests that many people try to encourage or persuade him.
  • 牛带竹笠 - Describes a cow wearing a bamboo hat, which is an unusual and humorous image.
  • 大如雨席 - Means 'as large as a rain umbrella,' emphasizing the size of the hat.
  • 仍牛前遣挝角 - Means 'and the cow is sent ahead to beat the horn,' which is a playful or absurd action.
  • 村【阙】城郭士女 - The text is incomplete, but it likely refers to villagers and city dwellers.
  • 谓之精怪 - Means 'they call it a spirit or monster.'
  • 每谒州郡骑 - Means 'every time he visits a state or prefecture, he rides a horse.'
  • 咍之者怒之者相半 - Means 'those who laugh at him and those who are angry with him are equally divided.'
  • 至则投刺其名 - Means 'when he arrives, he presents his name card.'
  • 衔【阙】 - The text is incomplete, but '衔' could refer to a bit or bridle used to control a horse.
  • 怜葱爱蒜 - Means 'to love scallions and garlic.'
  • 不得已而【阙】 - The text is incomplete, but it suggests a situation where he has no choice but to do something.
  • 悬宰视之如土木 - Means 'he hangs the butcher and treats him as if he were made of wood and stone.'
  • 藩帅郡侯柰 - Refers to high-ranking officials such as military governors and prefects, indicating that he is dealing with important figures.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 太平广记 - A comprehensive collection of stories and anecdotes from various historical periods, often containing supernatural and legendary elements.

  • 刺郡者亦是朝僚 - Those who submit petitions to the provincial governor are also officials of the court, indicating a hierarchical and bureaucratic system.

  • 牛带竹笠 - A humorous and unusual image of a cow wearing a bamboo hat.
  • 大如雨席 - Describing the size of the hat as large as a rain umbrella.
  • 仍牛前遣挝角 - A playful or absurd action where the cow is sent ahead to beat the horn.
  • 村【阙】城郭士女 - The text is incomplete, but it likely refers to villagers and city dwellers.
  • 谓之精怪 - They call it a spirit or monster, indicating a superstitious or folkloric interpretation.
  • 每谒州郡骑 - Every time he visits a state or prefecture, he rides a horse, suggesting a routine or ritual.
  • 咍之者怒之者相半 - Those who laugh at him and those who are angry with him are equally divided, indicating a mixed reaction.
  • 至则投刺其名 - When he arrives, he presents his name card, a formal custom in traditional Chinese society.
  • 衔【阙】 - The text is incomplete, but '衔' could refer to a bit or bridle used to control a horse.
  • 怜葱爱蒜 - Loving scallions and garlic, suggesting a preference for certain foods.
  • 不得已而【阙】 - The text is incomplete, but it suggests a situation where he has no choice but to do something.
  • 悬宰视之如土木 - Hanging the butcher and treating him as if he were made of wood and stone, indicating a cruel or inhumane action.
  • 藩帅郡侯柰 - High-ranking officials such as military governors and prefects, indicating that he is dealing with important figures.

Proper Nouns

  • 太平广记 - A famous Chinese collection of stories and anecdotes from various historical periods.
  • 嗤鄙五 - A section title in the *Taiping Guangji*.
  • 崔育 - A person's name, likely a candidate who has passed the imperial examination.
  • 唐 - The Tang Dynasty, a period known for its cultural and literary achievements.
  • 前进士 - A title indicating a candidate who has passed the imperial examination but has not yet been officially appointed as an official.
  • 中原乱离 - A period of chaos and disorder in the Central Plains.
  • 边上 - The frontier or border region.
  • 刺郡者 - Those who submit petitions to the provincial governor.
  • 朝僚 - Officials of the court.
  • 牛带竹笠 - A cow wearing a bamboo hat.
  • 大如雨席 - As large as a rain umbrella.
  • 仍牛前遣挝角 - The cow is sent ahead to beat the horn.
  • 村【阙】城郭士女 - Villagers and city dwellers.
  • 谓之精怪 - They call it a spirit or monster.
  • 每谒州郡骑 - Every time he visits a state or prefecture, he rides a horse.
  • 咍之者怒之者相半 - Those who laugh at him and those who are angry with him are equally divided.
  • 至则投刺其名 - When he arrives, he presents his name card.
  • 衔【阙】 - The text is incomplete, but '衔' could refer to a bit or bridle used to control a horse.
  • 怜葱爱蒜 - Loving scallions and garlic.
  • 不得已而【阙】 - The text is incomplete, but it suggests a situation where he has no choice but to do something.
  • 悬宰视之如土木 - Hanging the butcher and treating him as if he were made of wood and stone.
  • 藩帅郡侯柰 - High-ranking officials such as military governors and prefects.

[3041]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 之不可【阙】 - Fragmentary phrase, possibly meaning 'it is not possible' or 'it cannot be.'
  • 州民脔其肉族其家葢轻薄之所致也【阙】 - Complex sentence fragment, likely meaning 'the people of the state cut up his flesh and destroyed his family, which was caused by his frivolity.'
  • 唐进士宇文翃深慕上科有女及笄国色朝中令及第者【阙】 - Fragmentary sentence, possibly meaning 'Mr. Yuwen Hóng, a Tang dynasty Jinshi, deeply admired the upper class and had a daughter who reached the age of marriage and was of extraordinary beauty, and the court ordered him to pass the examination.'
  • 余方谋继室兄回为谏议【阙】 - Fragmentary sentence, possibly meaning 'I was planning to marry my brother back as a censor.'
  • 遂娉女与璠为言于回矣【阙】 - Fragmentary sentence, possibly meaning 'So I arranged for the daughter to marry Fan and spoke to him about it.'
  • 滑台杜志名时有丧遭火几棺柩【阙】 - Fragmentary sentence, possibly meaning 'Du Zhiming of Huatai had a funeral and a fire almost destroyed the coffin.'
  • 因而延燎杜谓宇文曰鱼将化龙【阙】有烧【阙】之事用以讥【阙出北梦琐言】 - Fragmentary sentence, possibly meaning 'As a result, the fire spread, and Du said to Yuwen, 'The fish is about to transform into a dragon,' referring to the burning incident, which was used to mock [something].'
  • 唐魏博节度使韩简性粗质每对文士不晓其说心常耻之乃召一孝防讲论语至为政篇翌日谓诸从事曰仆近方知古人淳朴年三十方能行立外有闻者无不絶倒【出北梦琐言】 - Fragmentary sentence, possibly meaning 'Mr. Han Jian, the Tang dynasty Jiedushi of Weibo, was coarse and simple-minded. When he was with scholars, he did not understand what they said, which often made him feel ashamed. So he called a filial and upright person to teach him the Analects of Confucius up to the 'Government' chapter. The next day, he told his subordinates, 'I have recently come to realize the simplicity of the ancients; they were not able to walk or stand until they were thirty.' Those who heard this outside could not help but laugh.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 唐进士 - Refers to a person who passed the imperial examination during the Tang dynasty.
  • 魏博节度使 - Refers to a military governor of the Weibo region during the Tang dynasty.
  • 孝防 - Refers to a filial and upright person.
  • 论语 - Refers to the Analects of Confucius, a classic Chinese text.
  • 为政篇 - Refers to the 'Government' chapter of the Analects of Confucius.
  • 奉先县 - Refers to Fengxian County, a place in Chinese history.
  • 邑寄 - Refers to a county magistrate.
  • 博奕 - Refers to gambling or playing chess.

Proper Nouns

  • 宇文翃 - A proper name, likely a person's surname.
  • 韩简 - A proper name, likely a person's surname.
  • 胡令 - A proper name, likely a person's surname.
  • 杜志名 - A proper name, likely a person's surname.
  • 北梦琐言 - A historical text, possibly a collection of anecdotes or gossip.

[3042]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 极是叨铁其义安在 - Metaphorical expression; '叨铁' (tāo tiě) likely refers to a specific action or behavior, and '其义安在' (qí yì ān zài) asks about the meaning or purpose of it.
  • 长官岂不知有叨铁 - Rhetorical question asking if the official doesn't know about '叨铁'.
  • 还见冶炉家置一铁长杖乎 - Question asking if the official has seen a long iron rod placed in a smelting furnace.
  • 只此是炉中猛火炎炽铁汁或未消融使此杖时时于炉中橦猛火了却出来移时又橦猛火了却出来只此是叨铁也 - Detailed description of the process of using a long iron rod in a furnace, which is metaphorically referred to as '叨铁'.
  • 言讫而去 - Means 'after saying this, he left.'
  • 胡入室话于妻子再三思之方知讽其每日自入噇猛火了却出来棋也 - Complex sentence where '胡' (hú) likely refers to a person, and '再三思之' (zài sān sī zhī) means 'after thinking about it again and again,' leading to the realization that the metaphor is about eating and playing chess.
  • 凡靳食倦客之士时人多以此讽之 - General statement that people often use this metaphor to criticize those who are stingy with food and lazy guests.
  • 出玉堂闲话 - Refers to a collection of anecdotes or stories.
  • 杨铮 - The name of a person.
  • 蜀秀才杨铮 - Refers to Yang Zheng, a scholar from Shu (Sichuan).
  • 行恶诗或故作落韵或丑语取人笑翫 - Describes Yang Zheng's habit of writing bad poetry or deliberately using improper rhymes to make people laugh.
  • 装修卷轴投谒王侯门到者无不逢迎雄藩大幕争驰车马迎之 - Describes how Yang Zheng, when visiting nobles, always received enthusiastic welcomes.
  • 铮每行仆马甚盛平头骑从骡携书袋偏郡小邑尤更精意承事之虑其谤讟 - Describes Yang Zheng's entourage and the efforts made by officials to please him.
  • 黔南节度使王茂权 - Refers to Wang Maowen, the governor of Qiannan.
  • 聪明有文武才四方负艺之士防不集其门 - Describes Wang Maowen as intelligent and talented in both literature and martial arts, attracting many talented people.
  • 召铮至饬东阁尽礼待之 - Wang Maowen invited Yang Zheng to his residence and treated him with great respect.
  • 时令贡恶诗以为欢笑 - Wang Maowen sometimes asked Yang Zheng to recite his bad poetry for entertainment.
  • 诸客请召有不得次者以为怏怏 - Other guests were unhappy because they did not get to be invited.
  • 茂权一日忽屏从谓之曰秀才客于当州必欲谘留相伴至罢镇同归可乎如可则当奉为卜娶所居奉留 - Wang Maowen privately asked Yang Zheng if he wanted to stay and get married after he finished his term.
  • 权令 - Wang Maowen ordered.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 叨铁 - Metaphorical term that needs to be explained in the context of the passage.
  • 冶炉家 - Refers to a smelting furnace, a common tool in ancient metallurgy.
  • 炉中猛火炎炽铁汁或未消融 - Describes the intense heat and molten iron in a furnace.
  • 玉堂闲话 - A collection of anecdotes or stories, often used in classical Chinese literature.
  • 蜀秀才 - Refers to a scholar from Sichuan, a region known for its cultural and intellectual contributions.
  • 恶诗 - Refers to bad poetry, often used to describe poetry that is intentionally poor or humorous.
  • 落韵 - Refers to improper rhyming in poetry.
  • 丑语 - Refers to vulgar or inappropriate language.
  • 装修卷轴投谒王侯门 - Describes the act of presenting a scroll of poetry to nobles, a common practice in ancient China.
  • 雄藩大幕 - Refers to powerful nobles or officials.
  • 黔南节度使 - Refers to the governor of Qiannan, a region in southern China.
  • 聪明有文武才 - Describes someone who is intelligent and talented in both literature and martial arts.
  • 四方负艺之士 - Refers to talented people from all over the country.
  • 东阁 - Refers to a guest hall or reception area in a noble's residence.
  • 贡恶诗 - Refers to the act of reciting bad poetry, often used for entertainment.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The long sequence '地斯须闷絶矣又作枷有十号棒名见即承复有铁圏笼头当讯囚圏中下楔其余名号数十大略如此也' is unclear. It could mean 'the ground is suddenly suffocated, another枷 with ten named rods, seeing it immediately confesses, then there is an iron ring and a head cage, when interrogating the prisoner in the circle, put a wedge, the rest of the names and numbers are roughly like this,' but the exact meaning is uncertain.
  • The phrase '囚人无贵贱必先列枷棒于地召囚前曰此是作具见之魂胆飞越无不自诬者' is unclear. It could mean 'prisoners, regardless of their status, must first place枷 and rods on the ground, calling the prisoner forward and saying, 'This is the tool for making, seeing the soul and courage fly away, no one does not confess on their own,' but the exact meaning is uncertain.
  • The phrase '则天重其爵赏以酬之故更竞劝为酷矣' means 'Empress Wu valued their titles and rewards to reward them, so they competed to encourage cruelty,' but the exact context is unclear.
  • The phrase '由是告宻之徒纷然道路名流僶俛阅日而已朝士因朝黙遭掩袭至于族灭与其家诀曰不知重相见否' means 'thus, those who report secrets were everywhere on the roads, famous people were forced to endure the days, court officials were suddenly attacked and their families were wiped out, they said goodbye to their families, saying, 'We don't know if we will meet again,' but the exact context is unclear.
  • The long sequence '天授中春官尚书狄仁杰天官侍郎任令晖文昌右丞卢献等五人并为其罗告俊臣既以族人家为功欲引人承反乃奏请降敕一问即承同首例得减死以脇仁杰等令承反杰欵曰大周革命万物惟新唐室旧臣甘从诛戮反是实' is unclear. It could mean 'in the middle of Tian Shou, the Spring Official尚书Dì Rénjié, the Celestial Official侍郎Rèn Lìnghuī, the文昌Right ChancellorLú Xiàn, and five others were all accused by them, Jùnchén, having made the family of the clan his achievement, wanted to lead people to confess to rebellion, so he petitioned to issue an edict, asking if they would confess, and if they did, they could be spared, to coerce Dì Rénjié and others to confess to rebellion, Jié said, 'The Great Zhou Revolution has renewed all things, the old ministers of the Tang court willingly accept punishment, and rebellion is indeed real,' but the exact meaning is uncertain.

  • The vivid description '以头触柱血流被面德夀惧而谢焉' needs to be rendered in a way that conveys the emotional impact.
  • The sentence '既承反所司待日行刑不复严防' describes a sequence of events that must be clearly articulated.
  • The complex sentence '得冯首者求笔砚折被头帛书之叙寃苦置于绵衣中遣谓德夀' must be broken down to maintain coherence.
  • The sentence '时方请赴家人去其緜' describes a request during an epidemic, requiring careful handling of the context.
  • The sentence '家人得衣中书杰子光逺持之称变得' describes the discovery of a letter by Di Renjie's family, requiring clear articulation.
  • The sequence of events '召见则天览之惘然召问俊臣' must be clearly described to maintain the narrative flow.

He burned them with fire until they could not bear the pain, and they made sounds like insects and birds day and night. Then he sent them to Moche, who should have them cut into pieces and killed. The Tujue people were resentful and dared not surrender.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 臣状不出上曰俊臣于国有功朕思之耳 - Refers to Emperor Zhongzong of Tang, who initially hesitated to punish Wei Junchen.
  • 顼奏曰于安远告虺贞反其事并验 - Refers to Xu, a minister who reported the treason of Huizhen.
  • 今贞为成州司马俊臣聚结不逞诬遘贤良赃贿如山寃魂满路国之贼也何足惜哉 - Refers to the political corruption and injustice under Emperor Zhongzong.
  • 上令状出诛俊臣于西市 - Refers to the execution of Wei Junchen in the Western Market.

  • 顼理綦连耀事以爲巳功授天官侍郎平章事 - Refers to Xu's taking credit for handling the case of Qi Lianyao.
  • 与河内王竞出为温州司马卒 - Refers to Xu's demotion and death.

  • 又取及鼈令人脱衣纵等囓其体终不肯放死而后已其人酸痛号呼不可复言王与姬妾共看以爲玩乐然后以竹刺鼈口遂囓竹而放人 - Refers to King Cheng's further cruel amusement.
  • 艾炙鼈背炙痛乃放口人被惊者皆失魂至死不平复矣 - Refers to the extreme cruelty and the psychological trauma inflicted on the victims.
  • 唐朔方总管张亶好杀时有突厥投化亶乃作檄文骂黙啜言词甚不逊书其腹背凿其肌肤湼之以墨炙之以火不胜楚痛日夜作虫鸟鸣然后送与黙啜字者宜讫脔而杀之匈奴怨望不敢降 - Refers to Zhang Dan's cruel treatment of Tujue prisoners.

[3119]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 贼臣 - A derogatory term for a traitorous minister, used to condemn someone who betrays their country or master.
  • 倡乱 - Literally 'initiate disorder,' meaning to start a rebellion or uprising.
  • 食人肝六十六 - A hyperbolic expression indicating that the person ate 66 human livers, emphasizing extreme cruelty.
  • 面剖而脍之 - A vivid description of how the liver was sliced and prepared, with '脍' referring to finely chopped meat.
  • 宛转呌呼 - Describes the victim's futile attempts to cry out or move, even as they are being killed.
  • 戮者人亦一二万 - Indicates that many people were killed, though the exact number is not specified.
  • 名帅仗皇威 - Refers to a famous general relying on the emperor's authority to suppress the rebellion.
  • 剪灭黔之猰防 - A metaphorical expression where '黔' refers to a mythical beast, and '猰防' is another mythical creature. Together, they symbolize the suppression of a dangerous and chaotic force.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 赵思绾 - A historical figure who led a rebellion during the Later Han period (947–951) in China.
  • 玉堂闲话 - A historical or literary work that records anecdotes and stories, often used as a reference for historical facts.
  • 安道进 - A historical figure, possibly a military officer or rebel, whose story is recounted in the text.
  • 庄宗 - Emperor Zhuangzong of Later Tang, a ruler of the Five Dynasties period (907–960).
  • 河东人 - Refers to someone from Hedong, a region in present-day Shanxi Province, historically known for its strategic importance.
  • 洞其七札 - A reference to a legendary feat of archery, where an arrow penetrates seven layers of armor, symbolizing exceptional skill or power.
  • 十札 - An extension of the '七札' metaphor, suggesting an even greater level of penetration or impact.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '贼臣赵思绾自倡乱至败凡食人肝六十六' is elliptical, omitting the subject and verb for the action of eating livers. It must be read as 'The traitorous minister Zhao Siwang, from the time he initiated disorder until his defeat, ate a total of 66 human livers.'
  • The phrase '无非面剖而脍之' modifies the previous action, indicating that the livers were not only eaten but also sliced and prepared in a specific way.
  • The clause '至食欲尽犹宛转呌呼而戮者人亦一二万' describes the victims' final moments, emphasizing their futile attempts to cry out or move even as they were killed.
  • The conditional clause '倘非名帅仗皇威而勦之则孰能剪灭黔之猰防' suggests that without the intervention of a famous general relying on the emperor's authority, no one could have suppressed the rebellion, which is likened to a mythical beast.

  • 安道进 - A historical figure, possibly a military officer or rebel.
  • 庄宗 - Emperor Zhuangzong of Later Tang.
  • 河东 - A historical region in present-day Shanxi Province.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 周迪妻 - Refers to the wife of Zhou Di, a historical figure. The title '妻' is a respectful way to refer to a wife in Classical Chinese.

  • 人相略卖 - Means 'people were captured and sold,' referring to the chaos during the rebellion.
  • 以食迪饥 - Means 'to feed Zhou Di when he was hungry.'

  • 见妻首已在于枅矣 - Means 'they saw that her head was already on the beam,' indicating the wife's execution.
  • 裹余体归塟之 - Means 'wrapped her remaining body and buried it,' showing the husband's grief and respect.
  • 邹待征妻 - Refers to the wife of Zou Dai Zheng, another historical figure.

  • 从待征官江隂 - Means 'followed Zou Dai Zheng to Jiangyin,' indicating her relocation.
  • 袁晁乱 - Refers to the rebellion led by Yuan Chao, another military leader during the late Tang Dynasty.
  • 解印窜匿 - Means 'unsealed his seal and went into hiding,' indicating Zou Dai Zheng's escape.
  • 将污之 - Means 'to defile her,' referring to the enemy's intention to violate her.
  • 不从 - Means 'did not comply,' showing her resistance.
  • 语家媪使报待征 - Means 'told the old woman to inform Zou Dai Zheng,' where '家媪' refers to a family servant.

  • 横行海浙江隂 - Means 'wreaking havoc in the sea and Jiangyin,' indicating the extent of the chaos.
  • 万戸化为凝血 - Means 'ten thousand households turned to congealed blood,' a hyperbolic expression of destruction.
  • 无石不焚无玉不折 - Means 'no stone was left unburned, no jade unbroken,' emphasizing the total devastation.
  • 峩峩薄媛 - Means 'the noble Lady Bo,' where '峩峩' is an honorific and '薄媛' refers to Lady Bo.

  • 自牧之子邹征之妻 - Means 'the daughter of Zimu, wife of Zou Zheng,' providing her lineage.
  • 玉德兰姿女之英兮 - Means 'with jade-like virtue and orchid-like grace, the flower of women,' praising her beauty and virtue.
  • 婉如之賔执为囚虏 - Means 'tender as a guest, yet seized as a prisoner,' contrasting her gentle nature with her fate.

  • 托垂白之姥姥 - Means 'entrusted to the old woman with white hair,' where '垂白' refers to an elderly person.
  • 兵解求尸在于江濵 - Means 'searched for her body along the riverbank,' indicating the efforts to find her.
  • 哀风起为连波 - Means 'grief arose, turning into waves,' a metaphor for the emotional impact.
  • 痛气结为孤云 - Means 'sorrow condensed into a solitary cloud,' another metaphor for grief.
  • 鳬雁为之哀鸣 - Means 'the wild ducks and geese mourned,' personifying nature's response.
  • 日月为之昬 - Means 'the sun and moon dimmed,' a metaphor for the darkness caused by her death.
  • 端标移景而恒 - Means 'her steadfast character shifted the scene and remained constant,' emphasizing her enduring influence.
  • 劲芳贯霜而犹存 - Means 'her strong fragrance penetrated the frost and still remained,' a metaphor for her lasting virtue.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 周迪妻某氏迪善贾往来广陵 - This sentence is elliptical and must be read as 'Zhou Di's wife, Lady Bo, was skilled in trade and traveled to Guangling.'
  • 毕师铎乱人相略卖以食迪饥 - This sentence is also elliptical and must be read as 'during Bi Shiduo's rebellion, people were captured and sold to feed Zhou Di when he was hungry.'
  • 君亲在不可并死愿见卖以济君行 - This sentence is elliptical and must be read as 'your parents are still alive, so we cannot die together; I wish to be sold to save you.'
  • 至城门守者谁何疑其绐与迪至肆问状见妻首已在于枅矣 - This sentence is complex and must be read as 'when they reached the city gate, the guards questioned them, suspecting them of deception, and when they went to the market to inquire, they saw that her head was already on the beam.'
  • 邹待征妻薄者武康尉自牧之女也从待征官江隂 - This sentence is elliptical and must be read as 'Lady Bo, the daughter of Wu Kang Wei Zimu, followed Zou Dai Zheng to Jiangyin.'
  • 袁晁乱解印窜匿将污之不从 - This sentence is elliptical and must be read as 'during Yuan Chao's rebellion, Zou Dai Zheng unsealed his seal and went into hiding, and the bandits intended to defile Lady Bo, but she did not comply.'
  • 语家媪使报待征我义不辱即死于水 - This sentence is elliptical and must be read as 'Lady Bo told the old woman to inform Zou Dai Zheng, saying, 'I would rather die in the water than be dishonored.'

  • 河池 - A town in Shaanxi Province during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 凤翔 - A prefecture in Shaanxi Province during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 秦帅李茂贞 - Li Maozhen, a military leader who controlled the region of Qin during the late Tang Dynasty.
  • 戎挍李继朗 - Li Jilang, a military officer under Li Maozhen.
  • 兖州 - A prefecture in Shandong Province during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 负担贩卖 - A common occupation for common people during the Tang Dynasty, involving carrying goods and trading.
  • 负担 - Refers to the practice of carrying goods for trade.
  • 贩卖 - Refers to the act of buying and selling goods.
  • 徃来于郡 - Indicates frequent travel between the prefectural capital and local areas.

[3140]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 才婦 - Refers to women of talent or ability.
  • 請與小郎解圍 - A polite request to intervene and help resolve a difficult situation.
  • 援筆立成 - Means she wrote something down quickly and completed it immediately.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 謝道韫 - A renowned woman of talent and virtue in the Eastern Jin dynasty, known for her literary skills and wisdom.
  • 王獻之 - A famous calligrapher and painter of the Eastern Jin dynasty, also known for his literary talents.
  • 上官昭容 - A prominent figure in the Tang dynasty, known for her literary and political influence.
  • 通天后 - Refers to the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, who ruled as the Empress Regnant of the Tang dynasty.
  • 景龍 - Refers to the early years of the Tang dynasty, specifically the reign of Emperor Zhongzong.

Proper Nouns

  • 謝道韫 - Xie Daojun, a renowned woman of talent and virtue in the Eastern Jin dynasty.
  • 王獻之 - Wang Xizhi, a famous calligrapher and painter of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
  • 上官昭容 - Empress Wu Zetian's niece, a prominent figure in the Tang dynasty.
  • 通天后 - The reign of Empress Wu Zetian.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 燕文贞公张説 - Refers to Zhang Yue, a prominent official during the Tang Dynasty, who was posthumously given the title 'Yan Wenzheng Gong' (文贞公).
  • 甞为舅求官 - Means 'once asked for an official position for his uncle.'
  • 父不语但指搘牀而示之 - Means 'the father did not speak but pointed to a turtle supporting the bed and showed it to her.'
  • 詹事 - An ancient Chinese official position, often translated as 'Grand Master of the Palace.'
  • 杜羔妻刘氏善为诗 - Means 'Liu, the wife of Du Gao, was skilled in poetry.'
  • 累举不中第 - Means 'failed repeatedly in the imperial examinations.'
  • 君到来时近夜来 - Means 'when you come back, it will be close to nightfall.'
  • 防昌中 - Refers to the time of the Tang Dynasty, specifically during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 邉将张暌防戍十有余年 - Means 'General Zhang Kui served on the border for over ten years.'
  • 绣回文作形诗 - Means 'wove a poem in the shape of a turtle.'
  • 诣阙进上诗 - Means 'presented the poem at the imperial court.'
  • 敇赐绢三百疋以彰才美 - Means 'was granted three hundred bolts of silk to commend her talent and beauty.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 燕文贞公张説 - Zhang Yue was a significant figure in the Tang Dynasty, known for his literary and political contributions.
  • 詹事 - The position of Grand Master of the Palace was a high-ranking official responsible for managing the imperial household.
  • 杜羔妻刘氏 - Liu, the wife of Du Gao, is a historical figure known for her poetic skills and support of her husband.
  • 防昌中 - The term '防昌中' refers to the period of the Tang Dynasty, particularly the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, known for its cultural and economic prosperity.
  • 邉将张暌 - General Zhang Kui was a military officer who served on the border for many years, facing the challenges of frontier defense.

Proper Nouns

  • 燕文贞公张説 - Zhang Yue, a prominent Tang Dynasty official.
  • 杜羔妻刘氏 - Liu, the wife of Du Gao.
  • 防昌中 - A period in the Tang Dynasty, specifically the reign of Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 邉将张暌 - General Zhang Kui, a military officer.
  • 侯氏 - Hou, the wife of General Zhang Kui.

[3142]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 闗圗有一妹甚聪恵文学书札防不动人 - Guan Tu has a younger sister who is very intelligent and talented in literature and calligraphy; her letters are well-received and respected.
  • 所恨不栉耳 - The only regret is that she is not married; a common expression indicating a desire for marriage.
  • 深结托圗 - He deeply relied on Guan Tu; indicates a strong trust and reliance.
  • 略晓文墨 - He has a slight knowledge of literature and calligraphy; suggests a basic understanding rather than expertise.
  • 才学优博 - His talent and learning are excellent and broad; emphasizes both breadth and depth of knowledge.
  • 越絶流辈 - He surpasses his peers; indicates exceptional ability.
  • 浮世到头须适性 - In this world, one must ultimately follow one's nature; reflects a philosophical view on life.
  • 男儿何必尽成功 - Why must a man achieve everything?; expresses a more relaxed attitude towards success.
  • 惟应鲍叔深知我 - Only Bo Shu truly understands me; alludes to the famous friendship between Guan Zhong and Bo Shu.
  • 他日蒲帆百尺风 - One day, with a hundred-foot sail in the wind; a metaphor for future success.
  • 又广陵秋夜读修所赋三篇复吟寄修 - Another night in Guangling, reading the three poems he wrote and reciting them to him; describes a poetic exchange.
  • 知输健笔时情谁不许高枝 - Knowing that a strong pen is needed, who would not allow a high branch?; suggests that one's talents should be recognized and utilized.
  • 明年二月东风里江岛闲人慰所思 - Next year in February, in the east wind, a leisurely person on the river island comforts his thoughts; describes a peaceful and reflective moment.
  • 闗氏乃与修读书习二十余年 - Guan's family studied and practiced with him for more than twenty years; indicates a long-term educational relationship.
  • 后修卒闗氏自为文祭之时人竞相冩 - After he died, Guan's family wrote an article to mourn him, and people competed to write about it; shows the impact and respect he received.
  • 出南楚新闻 - From the Southern Chu News; indicates the source of the information.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 闗圗 - Guan Tu, a historical figure known for his trustworthiness.
  • 鹾贾 - Salt merchant, an important economic role in ancient China.
  • 司空李公蔚 - Li Wei, the chief examiner who presided over the imperial examination.
  • 罗 - Luo Ying, a poet who failed the imperial examination.
  • 鲍叔 - Bo Shu, a historical figure known for his friendship with Guan Zhong.
  • 蒲帆百尺风 - A metaphor for future success, often used in classical literature.
  • 广陵 - Guangling, an ancient city in present-day Yangzhou, Jiangsu Province.
  • 南楚新闻 - A historical document or news report from the southern part of Chu State.

Proper Nouns

  • 闗圗 - Guan Tu
  • 鹾贾 - Salt merchant
  • 司空李公蔚 - Li Wei
  • 罗 - Luo Ying
  • 鲍叔 - Bo Shu
  • 广陵 - Guangling
  • 南楚新闻 - Southern Chu News

[3143]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 甚有才思 - A concise way of saying that she was very talented and had a good mind.
  • 有集行于世 - Had a collection of works circulated in the world.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 李亿 - A Tang Dynasty official, to whom fish Xuanji was once married.
  • 咸宜观 - A Taoist temple in Chang'an (modern-day Xi'an), where fish Xuanji lived after leaving her marriage.
  • 易求无价寳难得有心郎 - A line from fish Xuanji's poetry, expressing the difficulty of finding a true lover.
  • 兰销歇归春圃杨栁东西绊客舟 - Another line from her poetry, describing the fleeting nature of beauty and the passage of time.
  • 温璋 - A Tang Dynasty official who executed fish Xuanji for the murder of her servant.

Proper Nouns

  • 李亿 - A Tang Dynasty official.
  • 咸宜观 - A Taoist temple in Chang'an.
  • 温璋 - A Tang Dynasty official who executed fish Xuanji.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 救疗有加无瘳 - Treatment is increased but there is no healing.
  • 假之为赋 - I borrow this as a poem.
  • 庶解疾焉 - Perhaps to alleviate illness.
  • 伊淑德之如此 - Such virtue.
  • 何故羸厥姿貌 - Why does one's appearance become weak?
  • 沮其精神 - Why is one's spirit depressed?
  • 憔悴衣巾 - Worn out by illness.
  • 子惟形兮是寄 - You only entrust your form.
  • 形与子兮相亲 - Form and you are close.
  • 何不诲之以崇德 - Why not teach virtue?
  • 教之以自伦 - Teach self-discipline.
  • 异莱妻之乐道 - Different from Lady Lai's enjoyment of the Way.
  • 殊鸿妇之安贫 - Different from Mrs. Hong's contentment with poverty.
  • 岂痼疾而无生頼 - Is it not a chronic illness without hope?
  • 頼将防贱而欲忘身 - Relying on low status and wanting to forget oneself.
  • 仆于是勃然而应曰 - I then replied abruptly.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 庄子 - Refers to *Zhuangzi*, a foundational text of Daoist philosophy.
  • 魍魉 - A mythical creature from Chinese folklore, often associated with water and spirits.
  • 夏鼎 - Refers to the bronze vessels of the Xia dynasty, symbolizing historical and cultural heritage.
  • 荘 - Likely a reference to *Zhuangzi*, a key text in Daoist philosophy.
  • 莱妻 - Refers to Lady Lai, a historical or literary figure known for her moral integrity.
  • 鸿妇 - Refers to Mrs. Hong, another historical or literary figure known for her virtue.

  • 庄子 - *Zhuangzi*, a foundational text of Daoist philosophy.

  • 荘 - Likely a reference to *Zhuangzi*.
  • 莱妻 - Lady Lai, a historical or literary figure.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 之不博何所谈之不长 - Without broad knowledge, how can one's discourse be extensive?
  • 夫影依日而生像因人而见 - Shadows depend on light to exist, and forms are seen by observers.
  • 岂言谈之足晓何节物之能辨 - Can speech alone convey understanding? Can one distinguish between different qualities?
  • 随晦明以兴灭逐形骸以迁变 - Shadows rise and fall with the changing light, and forms change with the body.
  • 以愚夫畏影而鄙之性以彰智者视隂而迟暮之心可见 - The ignorant fear shadows and show their contempt, while the wise see shadows as a sign of aging.
  • 伊羙恶兮由巳影何辜而遇谴 - Beauty and ugliness are subjective; what fault do shadows have to be blamed?
  • 且予闻至道之精兮冥至道之极昏兮黙 - I have heard that the highest truth is profound and mysterious, and its ultimate nature is dark and silent.
  • 达人委性命之修短君子任时运之通塞 - Wise people accept their fate, and virtuous men endure the ups and downs of life.
  • 悔吝不能纒荣耀不能惑防之不以爲防得之不以爲得 - Misfortune cannot entangle them, and glory cannot confuse them. They do not regard misfortune as misfortune and do not consider gain as gain.
  • 君子何乃怒予之不赏芳春责予之不贵华餙 - Why should the wise be angry that I do not appreciate the beauty of spring or value external adornment?
  • 且吾之秉操奚子智之能测 - And how can one's true character be measured by human wisdom?
  • 言未卒魍魉惕然而惊叹而起曰仆生絶域之外长于荒遐之境未晓智者之处身是以造君而问影 - Before I finished speaking, a ghost was startled and exclaimed, 'I was born in a distant land and grew up in a remote place. I do not understand how the wise live. Therefore, I came to ask you about shadows.'
  • 既谈之至妙请终身以藏屏 - After discussing the profound wisdom, I requested that it be kept secret and hidden forever.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 至道 - The highest truth or ultimate principle, often associated with Daoist philosophy.
  • 达人 - Wise or enlightened individuals who have achieved a high level of understanding.
  • 君子 - A person of virtue and moral integrity, often used in Confucian philosophy.
  • 魍魉 - Ghosts or spirits, often associated with the supernatural in Chinese folklore.
  • 絶域之外 - Remote or distant places, often used to describe regions beyond the known world.
  • 荒遐之境 - Remote or distant lands, often used to describe areas far from civilization.

[3146]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 愼氏北陵防亭儒家之女也 - Classical Chinese sentence structure beginning with the subject and ending with '也' (also), indicating a declarative statement.
  • 拾其过而出妻 - '拾其过' means 'to find fault with her' or 'to accuse her'; '出妻' is an archaic term for 'divorcing one’s wife.'
  • 雨防云飞一饷间 - Poetic line; '雨防' may be a phonetic or poetic variation of '雨散' (rain disperses), '云飞' (clouds fly), '一饷间' means 'in a moment.'
  • 不堪重上望夫山 - '望夫山' is a well-known Chinese legend of a wife waiting for her husband; the phrase suggests emotional exhaustion or despair.
  • 求道青城访僧衡岳 - '青城' refers to Qingcheng Mountain, a famous Taoist site in Sichuan; '衡岳' is Hengshan Mountain, one of the Five Sacred Mountains of China.
  • 对镜画其形并诗四韵以寄之 - '四韵' refers to a regulated verse form with four lines, each with a rhyme.
  • 欲下丹青笔先拈寳镜端 - '丹青' refers to painting; '寳镜' is a mirror; the line suggests the act of painting from a mirror, a common poetic motif in Chinese literature.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 云谿友议 - Title of the source text, a collection of anecdotes and stories from the Tang Dynasty; should be translated as *Yunxi Youyi* or *Friends of the Cloud Stream* with a note.
  • 望夫山 - Reference to the legend of a wife waiting for her husband; can be translated as *Mount of the Waiting Wife* or *Mount Waiting Wife* with a brief explanation.

Proper Nouns

  • 愼氏 - A woman’s name; keep consistent as Shen Shi.
  • 严灌夫 - A man’s name; keep consistent as Yan Guanfu.
  • 薛媛 - A woman’s name; keep consistent as Xue Yuan.
  • 南楚材 - A man’s name; keep consistent as Nan Chucai.
  • 颍 - A region in ancient China; modern-day Henan.
  • 青城 - Qingcheng Mountain, a Taoist center in Sichuan.
  • 衡岳 - Hengshan Mountain, one of the Five Sacred Mountains.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 内治 - Classical term meaning 'internal governance' or 'domestic affairs,' often used to refer to a woman's role in managing the household.
  • 才思非妇人之事 - Phrase suggesting that creative thinking or literary talent is not considered appropriate for women in the context of the time.
  • 白蜡烛 - Refers to a white candle, likely a metaphorical or symbolic object in the poem.
  • 紫禁 - Refers to the Forbidden City, the imperial palace, symbolizing the highest level of authority and power.
  • 天书 - Refers to imperial edicts or important writings, often associated with divine or sacred texts.
  • 绿窻风月 - Poetic phrase that evokes a serene, elegant setting, often associated with a refined or cultured environment.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 孙氏 - Refers to the Sun family, a prominent family in Chinese history, particularly during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 乐昌孙氏 - Specifically refers to the Sun family from Lechang, a region in present-day Guangdong Province, known for its cultural and literary contributions.
  • 进士 - A degree in the imperial examination system, indicating a high level of education and social status.
  • 白蜡烛 - The candle is likely a metaphor for purity, clarity, or enlightenment, often used in classical Chinese poetry.
  • 紫禁 - The Forbidden City, the imperial palace in Beijing, symbolizes the emperor's authority and the highest level of power.
  • 天书 - Imperial edicts or writings, often associated with divine or sacred texts, emphasizing the importance of the written word in governance and culture.
  • 绿窻风月 - A poetic phrase that evokes a refined, cultured setting, often associated with the life of a scholar or literati.

Proper Nouns

  • 孙氏 - The Sun family, a prominent family in Chinese history.
  • 乐昌孙氏 - The Sun family from Lechang, a region in present-day Guangdong Province.
  • 孟昌期 - A scholar-official (jinshi) from the Sun family.
  • 紫禁 - The Forbidden City, the imperial palace in Beijing.
  • 天书 - Imperial edicts or writings, often associated with divine or sacred texts.
  • 绿窻风月 - A poetic phrase that evokes a refined, cultured setting.

[3148]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 越謀滅吳 - The Yue plotted to destroy Wu, indicating a strategic plan by the state of Yue to conquer the state of Wu.
  • 畜天下竒寶美人異味 - Accumulated the world's rare treasures, beautiful women, and exotic delicacies, suggesting the collection of valuable items and people.
  • 進于吳 - Offered to Wu, indicating the act of presenting these items to the state of Wu.
  • 得隂峰之瑶古皇之骥湘沅之鱓 - Obtained the jade from the mountain peaks, the ancient emperor's steed, and the eels from the Xiang and Yuan rivers, listing specific rare items.
  • 又有美女一名夷光一名修明以贡于吴 - There were also two beautiful women named Yiguang and Xioming who were offered to Wu, introducing the names of the women.
  • 吴处于椒花之房贯细珠以为帘幌朝下以蔽景夕卷以待月 - The Wu king lived in a room adorned with pepper flowers and fine pearls strung as curtains, which were lowered during the day to block the sunlight and raised at night to welcome the moon, describing the luxurious living conditions.
  • 二人当轩并坐理镜靓妆于珠幌之内 - The two women sat side by side in front of the window, arranging their mirrors and adorning themselves within the pearl curtains, depicting a scene of beauty and elegance.
  • 窃窥者莫不动心惊魂谓之神人 - Those who secretly watched were all moved to their core and called them divine beings, indicating the mesmerizing effect of the women's beauty.
  • 吴王夫差目之若双鸾之在轻雾沚水之漾秋蕖 - King Fuchai of Wu regarded them as two phoenixes in light mist and lotus flowers floating on the water, comparing the women to mythical birds and flowers.
  • 妖惑既深怠于国政 - Their enchantment was so deep that he neglected his state affairs, indicating the negative impact of the women's beauty on the king's governance.
  • 及越兵入国乃抱二人以逃吴苑 - When the Yue army invaded the country, he took the two women and fled to the Wu palace, showing the king's reaction to the invasion.
  • 越军既入见二人在竹树下皆言神女望而不侵 - When the Yue army entered, they saw the two women under the bamboo and trees, and everyone said they were divine maidens, gazing without being touched, describing the women's appearance after the invasion.
  • 今吴城虵门内有折株尚为祠神女之处 - Now, inside the snake gate of Wu city, there is a broken branch that still serves as a place to worship the divine maiden, indicating a lasting legacy of the women's presence.
  • 汉武帝所幸宫人名曰丽娟年始十四玉肤柔软吹气如兰身轻弱不欲衣缨拂恐伤为痕 - A woman named Lijuan, favored by Emperor Wu of Han, was only fourteen years old. She had soft jade-like skin, her breath smelled like orchids, and she was so light and delicate that even the slightest touch of her clothing could leave a mark, describing her physical characteristics.
  • 每歌李延年和之于【阙】芝生殿旁唱回风之曲庭中树为之翻落 - Whenever she sang, Li Yanian would harmonize with her, and the chrysanthemum growing near the palace would sing the 'Return Wind' song, causing the trees in the courtyard to fall, describing a magical scene.
  • 常致娟于琉璃帐恐垢汚体也常以衣带系娟袂闭于重幕中恐随风起 - He always kept Lijuan in a glass tent to protect her from dirt, and often tied her sleeves with his clothes to prevent her from being carried away by the wind, showing the king's care for her.
  • 娟以琥珀为佩置衣裾里不使人知乃言骨节自鸣相与为神怪也 - Lijuan wore an amber pendant hidden in her clothes, and when she moved, it would make a sound, creating a mysterious and supernatural effect, adding to her mystique.
  • 汉赵飞鷰体轻腰弱善行步进退女弟昭仪不能及也 - Zhao Feiyan had a light and weak body, and she was skilled in walking forward and backward, something her sister, the Empress, could not match, describing her physical grace.
  • 但弱骨丰肌工笑语二人并色如红玉当时第一擅殊宠后宫 - She had delicate bones and full flesh, was skilled in smiling and speaking, and both she and her sister had faces like red jade, making them the most beautiful and favored in the palace, emphasizing their beauty and status.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 越謀滅吳 - Refers to the historical conflict between the states of Yue and Wu during the Spring and Autumn period.
  • 吴王夫差 - King Fuchai of Wu, a historical figure known for his defeat by the Yue.
  • 椒花之房 - A luxurious room adorned with pepper flowers, symbolizing wealth and luxury.
  • 细珠以为帘幌 - Fine pearls strung as curtains, indicating opulence and refinement.
  • 汉武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, a powerful ruler of the Han dynasty.
  • 李延年 - A famous musician and singer in the Han dynasty, known for his musical talents.
  • 回风之曲 - A song that caused trees to fall, a magical and supernatural event.
  • 琉璃帐 - A glass tent, symbolizing protection and luxury.
  • 琥珀为佩 - An amber pendant, a symbol of elegance and mystery.
  • 赵飞鷰 - Zhao Feiyan, a historical figure known for her beauty and grace.
  • 昭仪 - The title of an imperial concubine, indicating a high rank in the palace hierarchy.
  • 薛灵芸 - Xue Lingyun, a historical figure known for her beauty and favor in the palace.

[3149]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 魏文帝所爱美人 - Refers to a beautiful woman favored by Emperor Wen of Wei.
  • 薛灵芸常山人也 - Indicates that the woman's name is Xue Lingyun and she is from Changshan.
  • 父名邺爲酂乡亭长 - Describes her father's name as Ye and his position as the chief of a small town in Canxiang.
  • 母陈氏随邺舍于亭傍居 - Mentions that her mother, Chen, lived with her father near the town hall.
  • 生穷贱 - Indicates that she was born into a poor family.
  • 至夜每聚邻妇以绩燃麻藁自照 - Describes the nightly gatherings of neighbors for spinning and lighting the fields with dried grass.
  • 灵芸年十五容貌絶世 - States that at the age of fifteen, she had an extraordinary appearance.
  • 闾中少年多以夜时来窥终不得见 - Describes how young men from the neighborhood would come to see her at night but never got to meet her.
  • 咸熙元年谷习出守常山郡 - Mentions the year Xinxi and the person Gu Xi who went to govern Changshan.
  • 闻亭长有美女而家甚贫时文帝选良家子女以入六宫习以千金寳赂聘之 - Describes how Gu Xi heard about a beautiful woman from a poor family and offered a large bribe to Emperor Wen to acquire her.
  • 既得便以献文帝 - States that after obtaining her, he presented her to Emperor Wen.
  • 灵芸闻别父母防欷累日泪下霑衣 - Describes how Lingyun wept for several days after hearing she would be separated from her parents.
  • 至升车就路之时以玉唾壶盛泪壶中即如红色 - Describes how she collected her tears in a jade spittoon, which turned red.
  • 既发常山及至京师壶中泪凝如血 - States that when she left Changshan and arrived in the capital, the tears in the spittoon had solidified like blood.
  • 帝遣车十乘以迎灵芸车皆镂金为轮丹画其毂轭前有杂寳为龙凤衔百子铃锵和鸣响于林野 - Describes the emperor sending ten carriages to welcome her, each with golden wheels, red-painted axles, and bells shaped like dragons and phoenixes.
  • 驾青色骈蹄之牛日行三百里 - Mentions the use of blue, paired-hoofed cattle that could travel 300 li in a day.
  • 此牛尸涂国所献足如马蹄也 - States that these cattle were a gift from the Shitu country and had hooves like horses.
  • 道侧烧石叶之香 - Describes the burning of fragrant stone leaves along the road.
  • 此石重叠状如云母其气辟恶厉之疾 - Mentions that these stones were layered and resembled mica, and their fragrance could ward off diseases.
  • 腹题国所献也 - States that these stones were a gift from the Futi country.
  • 灵芸未至京师数十里膏烛之光相续不灭 - Describes how the light from candles and oil lamps continued for dozens of miles before reaching the capital.
  • 车从噎路尘起蔽于星月 - Mentions that the dust from the carriages obscured the stars and moon.
  • 又筑土为台基高三十丈列烛于台下而名曰烛台远望如列星之坠地 - Describes the construction of a high platform with candles arranged at its base, which was called the 'Candle Platform' and looked like falling stars from afar.
  • 又于大道之傍一里致一铜表高五尺以志里数故行者歌曰青槐夹道多尘埃龙楼凤阁望崔嵬清风细雨杂香来土上出金火照台 - Mentions the placement of copper markers every li along the road, each five feet tall, and the song sung by travelers.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 時爲銅柱以志里數於道側 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'at that time, copper pillars were erected on the roadside to mark the distance in li.' Reflects a practical method of measuring and marking distances in ancient times.
  • 土上出金 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'gold emerging from the earth,' symbolizing wealth and prosperity.
  • 火在土下之義 - Symbolic phrase meaning 'the meaning of fire beneath the earth,' possibly referring to the cyclical nature of elements or the transition of power.
  • 靈芸未至京師十里帝乘雕玉之辇以望車徒之盛 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'before Ling Yun arrived at the capital city, the emperor rode in a carved jade chariot to see the grand procession.' Highlights the emperor's anticipation and the grandeur of the event.
  • 因改靈芸之名爲夜來 - Literal phrase meaning 'therefore, he changed Ling Yun's name to Night Comes,' which is a poetic and symbolic renaming.
  • 入宮承寵愛 - Phrase meaning 'entered the palace to receive favor and love,' indicating the queen's high status and the emperor's affection.
  • 明珠翡翠尚不勝況乎龍鸾之重 - Comparative phrase meaning 'even precious pearls and emeralds are not enough, let alone the weight of dragon phoenix hairpins,' emphasizing the value and significance of the gifts.
  • 乃止而不進 - Literal phrase meaning 'he stopped and did not proceed,' indicating the emperor's reaction to the heavy gifts.
  • 夜來妙於女功 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'Night Comes was skilled in needlework,' highlighting her exceptional talent.
  • 雖處於深帷重幄之内不用燈燭裁制立成 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'even when she was in the depths of a heavy curtain and did not use candles, she could complete her work instantly,' emphasizing her skill and efficiency.
  • 非夜來所縫制帝不服也 - Literal phrase meaning 'if it was not made by Night Comes, the emperor would not wear it,' indicating her unparalleled skill.
  • 宮中號曰針神 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'she was called the needle god in the palace,' highlighting her reputation and skill.
  • 孫亮姬朝姝 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'Sun Liang's concubine, Morning Beauty,' introducing a new character.
  • 四姬皆振古絶色 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the four concubines were all of ancient unparalleled beauty,' emphasizing their exceptional beauty.
  • 一名朝姝二名麗居三名洛珍四名潔華 - List of names, each with a descriptive meaning: 'Morning Beauty,' 'Beautiful Abode,' 'Luoyang Treasure,' and 'Pure Splendor.'
  • 使四人坐屏風内而外望之如無隔唯香气不通于外 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'he had the four concubines sit inside the screen, and from the outside, it looked as if there was no barrier, but the fragrance did not pass through,' highlighting the screen's transparency and the concubines' presence.
  • 爲四人合四氣香 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'he made four fragrances for the four concubines,' indicating the personalized nature of the perfumes.
  • 此香殊方异国所獻 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'this fragrance was presented by distant and foreign countries,' emphasizing the exotic origin of the perfumes.
  • 凡經歲践蹑宴息之处香气沾衣歴年弥盛百浣不歇 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'after a year, the fragrance would adhere to the clothes worn in the place where they walked and rested, becoming more intense over time and not fading even after a hundred washes,' highlighting the lasting nature of the fragrance.
  • 因名百濯香 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'therefore, it was named the Hundred Wash Perfume,' indicating the fragrance's durability.
  • 或以人名香故有朝姝麗居洛珍潔華香 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'or named after the people, hence the perfumes of Morning Beauty, Beautiful Abode, Luoyang Treasure, and Pure Splendor,' emphasizing the personal connection to the concubines.
  • 亮每游此四人皆同与席 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'Sun Liang would often visit, and the four concubines would always be present,' indicating the regularity of his visits.
  • 使來侍皆以香名前后爲次不得相乱 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'he had them serve in order of their fragrance names, without mixing them up,' emphasizing the organized and respectful treatment of the concubines.
  • 所居室爲思香媚寝 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the room where they stayed was called the Fragrant and Lovely Rest,' highlighting the pleasant and fragrant environment.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 靈芸 - Refers to a historical figure, possibly a concubine or queen during the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 夜來 - Symbolic name meaning 'Night Comes,' which may have a deeper cultural or symbolic significance.
  • 火珠龍鸾之钗 - Refers to valuable and exotic gifts, including a fire pearl and dragon phoenix hairpins, which were highly prized in ancient times.
  • 百濯香 - A type of perfume that retains its fragrance even after multiple washes, symbolizing purity and longevity.
  • 思香媚寝 - A room name that suggests a fragrant and pleasant resting place, reflecting the high status and comfort of the concubines.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 当指白日以汝为殉 - Literal meaning 'to point to the bright day and make you a sacrifice,' likely refers to a symbolic or ritual act of devotion or loyalty.
  • 生爱死离不如无爱 - Philosophical statement suggesting that the pain of parting at death is worse than not having love in the first place.
  • 妾得为殉身其何朽 - Rhetorical question expressing that if she can be a sacrifice, her body will not decay.
  • 弥见宠爱 - Means 'even more loved' or 'increasingly favored.'
  • 崇常择美容姿相类者数十人装饰衣服大小一等 - Describes a practice of selecting many beautiful women who resemble each other and dressing them in identical outfits.
  • 使忽视不相分别 - Means 'to make them indistinguishable from one another.'
  • 常侍于侧 - Means 'to always serve at her side.'
  • 使翾风调玉以付工人为倒龙之佩萦金为凤冠之钗 - Describes the crafting of intricate jewelry, specifically a 'reversed dragon' pendant and a 'golden phoenix' hairpin.
  • 刻玉为倒龙之势铸金像凤凰之形 - Describes the carving of jade into a 'reversed dragon' shape and casting gold into the form of a 'phoenix.'
  • 结袖绕楹而舞昼夜相接谓之常舞 - Describes a continuous dance performed by the women, involving tying their sleeves together and dancing around pillars.
  • 有所召者不呼姓名悉听佩声视钗色玉声轻者居前金色艶者居后以爲行次而进也 - Describes a system where the order of entry is determined by the sound of the jade pendants and the color of the hairpins, with lighter jade sounds preceding and more vibrant gold following.
  • 使数十人各含异香使行而笑语则口气从风而飏 - Describes a practice where the women carry different fragrances, and when they speak, the scents are carried by the wind.
  • 又屑沈水之香如尘末布致象牀上使所爱践之无迹即赐珍珠百琲若有迹者则节其饮食令体轻弱 - Describes a test where the women walk on a bed covered with finely ground sandalwood, and those who leave no trace are rewarded with pearls, while those who do are punished by reducing their food intake.
  • 石氏房中并歌此为乐曲至晋末乃止 - Indicates that this practice was performed in the rooms of the Shi family and became a musical entertainment that continued until the end of the Jin dynasty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 妬忌 - Compound meaning 'jealous and suspicious,' describing the character trait of the wife, Segment Mingguang.
  • 取婦得如此 - Colloquial expression meaning 'to have a wife like this,' referring to the ideal of a beautiful and virtuous wife.
  • 君何得以水神美而欲輕我 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Why do you want to belittle me because of the beauty of a water deity?' This phrase highlights the wife's jealousy and her desire for recognition.
  • 吾死何患不爲水神 - Statement expressing the wife's determination to become a water deity after death, reflecting her strong will and desire for vengeance.
  • 壞衣枉妆 - Colloquial expression meaning 'to ruin clothes and make a pretense of beauty,' describing the actions of women who cross the river to avoid the deity's jealousy.
  • 丑妇虽盛妆饰而渡 - Phrase meaning 'even if an ugly woman is well-dressed, she can still cross the river,' indicating that the deity's jealousy is not limited to physical beauty.
  • 欲求好妇立在津口 - Colloquial expression meaning 'to find a good wife, stand at the ferry crossing,' suggesting that the ferry crossing is a place where one can find a good wife.
  • 妇人水旁好丑自彰 - Phrase meaning 'the beauty or ugliness of women is evident near the water,' emphasizing the transparency of women's qualities in the presence of the deity.
  • 妬记 - Term referring to a collection of stories about jealousy, which provides the context for the narrative.

  • 齐人语 - Refers to a proverb or saying from the state of Qi, providing a cultural context for the story.

[3156]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 杜兰香降张硕 - Supernatural or divine intervention, where '杜兰香' (Duran Xiang) refers to a deity or supernatural being appearing to '张硕' (Ou).
  • 硕妻无子取妾 - Ou's wife had no children and took a concubine. '取妾' (took a concubine) implies a social context where having a concubine was common.
  • 硕谓香如此云何 - Ou asked Xiang what to do. '云何' (what to do) is a classical way of asking for advice or a solution.
  • 香曰此易治耳 - Xiang said it was easy to fix. '易治耳' (easy to fix) is a colloquial expression conveying a sense of ease.
  • 言卒而硕妻患创委顿 - After saying this, Ou's wife suffered from an illness and became weak. '患创委顿' (suffered from an illness and became weak) is a vivid description of the wife's condition.
  • 此创所以治妬创已亦当瘥 - This illness is to treat jealousy, and it will also heal. '治妬' (to treat jealousy) is a metaphorical way of addressing a problem.
  • 数日之间创损而妻无妬心遂生数男 - In a few days, the illness subsided, and the wife lost her jealous heart, giving birth to several sons. '无妬心' (lost her jealous heart) is a metaphorical expression.
  • 唐初兵部尚书任环敕赐宫女二人皆国色妻妬烂二女头髪秃尽 - In the early Tang Dynasty, the Minister of War Ren Huan was granted two beautiful palace girls by imperial decree, but his wife, out of jealousy, caused their hair to fall out. '国色' (beautiful) and '头髪秃尽' (hair fell out) are vivid descriptions.
  • 太宗闻之令上宫赍金胡缾酒赐之云饮之立死 - Emperor Taizong heard about it and ordered the palace to bring a golden trinket and wine, saying that if she drank it, she would die immediately. '立死' (die immediately) is a dramatic expression.
  • 环三品合置姬媵尔后不妬不须饮之妬即饮 - Ren, being a third-rank official, should have concubines. If she does not become jealous, she does not need to drink; if she does, she should drink. '不妬不须饮之' (does not need to drink) and '妬即饮' (should drink) are conditional statements.
  • 又房龄夫人至妬太宗将赐美人屡辞不受乃令皇后召夫人语以媵妾之流令有常制且司空年近迟暮帝欲有优崇之意夫人执心不廻帝乃令谓曰宁不妬而生宁妬而死曰妾宁妬而死乃遣酌一巵酒与之曰然可饮此一酖一举便尽无所留难帝曰我尚畏见何况于龄乎 - Again, Fang Xuanling's wife was extremely jealous. Emperor Taizong wanted to grant her a beauty but she refused. He then ordered the Empress to call the wife and explain that having concubines was a common practice, especially since Fang was an elderly statesman. The wife refused to change her mind and said, 'I would rather die without jealousy than live with him.' She was given a cup of wine and said, 'If you want me to die, here it is.' She drank it all without hesitation. Emperor Taizong said, 'Even I fear her. How much more so Fang Xuanling?' '至妬' (extremely jealous) and '宁不妬而生宁妬而死' (rather die without jealousy than live with him) are vivid expressions of her jealousy.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 莫怪诗成无泪滴 - Reference to the emotional numbness or the difficulty of expressing deep feelings, often found in Chinese literature.
  • 尽倾东海也须干 - Reference to the depth of sorrow or the futility of trying to measure it, often found in Chinese literature.

  • 姮娥 - The moon goddess, often associated with the moon and the night.
  • 纱窻 - Distant window, often associated with longing and remembrance.
  • 书幌 - Book curtain, often associated with the solitude of the writer.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 青鸟罢衔牋金盆已覆难收 - This phrase is a compound metaphor, combining the cessation of hope with the irretrievable loss of something.
  • 水玉轸长抛不续 - This phrase is a compound metaphor, combining the abandonment of something with the inability to restore it.
  • 蘼芜山下过遥将红泪洒穷泉 - This phrase is a compound metaphor, combining the passing by a place of sorrow with the shedding of tears for the dead.
  • 鸾飞远树栖何处凤得新巢已去心 - This phrase is a compound metaphor, combining the loss of something beautiful with the change of heart or a new beginning.
  • 红璧尚留香漠漠碧云初散信沈沈 - This phrase is a compound metaphor, combining the enduring value of something with the fading of hope.
  • 情知防汚投泥玉犹自经营买笑金 - This phrase is a compound metaphor, combining the vulnerability of purity with the pursuit of fleeting happiness.
  • 从此山头似人石丈夫形状泪痕深 - This phrase is a compound metaphor, combining the permanence of memory or the enduring nature of certain things with profound sorrow or suffering.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 韦蟾亷问鄂州及罢任賔僚盛陈祖席 - Describes an event where Wei Chan, a high-ranking official, visited Ezhou (modern-day Wuchang) and was entertained by his subordinates upon his departure. '盛陈祖席' means 'lavishly prepared farewell banquet.'
  • 悲莫悲兮生别离 登山临水送将归 - Two lines from the *Wen Xuan* (Selections of Refined Literature). The first line expresses the sorrow of parting, and the second line describes the act of seeing someone off by climbing a mountain or standing by water.
  • 某不才不敢染翰欲口占两句 - A humble expression used by a woman, indicating that she is not skilled enough to write but wishes to contribute two lines orally.
  • 韦大惊异令随口写之 - '韦大惊异' means 'Wei was greatly surprised,' and '令随口写之' means 'he ordered her to write them down immediately.'
  • 武昌无限新栽桞 不见杨花扑面飞 - The two lines contributed by the woman, which Wei Chan found so impressive that he had them written down. They describe the beauty of newly planted willows in Wuchang and the absence of willow catkins (杨花) flying around.
  • 赠数十笺纳之翌日共载而发 - '赠数十笺纳之' means 'he gave her dozens of sheets of paper to keep,' and '翌日共载而发' means 'the next day, they set off together.'
  • 韦保衡尝访同人方坐李钜新及第亦继至 - Describes an event where Wei Baoqing visited a gathering where Li Ju, who had just passed the imperial examinations, arrived later.
  • 有客乎同人曰韦保衡秀才可以出否 - A question posed by the host, asking if Wei Baoqing, who was considered talented, could come out from hiding.
  • 徐曰出也何妨保衡竟不之出 - '徐曰出也何妨' means 'slowly said, 'It’s fine if he comes out,' and '保衡竟不之出' means 'but Wei Baoqing did not come out.'
  • 李蠙镇岐下钜方自山北旧从事辟焉 - Describes Li Qin, who was stationed in Qishui (modern-day Gansu), and Li Ju, who was appointed by him.
  • 独孤云除东川辟在幕下乐籍间有佐饮者副使李甲属意也 - Describes a situation where Du Gu Yun, a subordinate of Wei Baoqing, had a woman in his retinue who was interested in Li Jia, a deputy.
  • 时以逼于他适私期廻将纳焉 - Means 'at that time, due to other commitments, they had privately agreed to meet and consummate their relationship.'
  • 保衡既至不知之祈于独孤且请降其籍 - Means 'when Wei Baoqing arrived, he did not know about this and asked Du Gu Yun to lower her status.'
  • 李至意殊不平每在宴席辄以语侵保衡 - Means 'Li Jia was very upset and often insulted Wei Baoqing during banquets.'
  • 保衡不能容即携其妓人以去 - Means 'Wei Baoqing could no longer tolerate this and took the woman away.'
  • 李益怒之屡言于云云不得已命飞牒追之而廻 - Means 'Li Jia became even angrier and repeatedly reported to Du Gu Yun, who, unable to do anything, ordered a fast dispatch to recall Wei Baoqing.'
  • 不日保衡复入翰林 - Means 'soon, Wei Baoqing returned to the Hanlin Academy.'
  • 李闻之登时而卒 - Means 'when Li Jia heard this, he died immediately.'

  • 王戎梦有人以七枚椹子与之着衣襟中 - A classical narrative structure describing a dream vision. '梦' means 'dream,' '有人' means 'someone,' '以七枚椹子与之' means 'gave seven plums to him,' and '着衣襟中' means 'in his collar.'
  • 既觉得之 - A common phrase meaning 'upon waking up, he realized it.'
  • 占曰 - Means 'interpreted' or 'interpreted as.'
  • 椹桑子也 - A pun or symbolic interpretation where '椹' (plum) and '桑' (mulberry) are both trees, and '子' means 'seed' or 'offspring.'
  • 自后男女大小凡七防 - '自后' means 'from then on,' '男女大小' means 'men and women of all ages,' '凡' means 'all,' and '七防' means 'seven burials.'
  • 邹湛梦一人拜自称甄仲舒求湛觉思之曰舍西瓦土中人也乃取之复梦其人来拜谢 - Another dream narrative. '拜' means 'bowed,' '自称' means 'called himself,' '甄仲舒' is a proper name, '求' means 'requested burial,' '觉思之' means 'upon waking, he thought about it,' '舍西瓦土中人也' means 'a person from the west of his house in the earthen mound,' '乃取之' means 'so he took him and buried him,' and '复梦其人来拜谢' means 'he dreamed again that the person came to bow and thank him.'
  • 虞翻注易上奏曰臣郡吏陈桃梦臣与道士相遇散髪麤裘付易六爻烧其三以饮臣臣乞尽吞之道士言易在天上三爻足以岂臣受命应当知也 - A complex narrative involving a dream and a divination ritual. '注易' means 'annotated the *I Ching*,' '上奏曰' means 'presented a report saying,' '臣郡吏陈桃梦臣与道士相遇' means 'my subordinate official Chen Tao dreamed that I met a Taoist priest,' '散髪麤裘' means 'with loose hair and rough robe,' '付易六爻' means 'gave the *I Ching* six lines,' '烧其三以饮臣' means 'burned three of them to drink me,' '臣乞尽吞之' means 'I begged to swallow them all,' '道士言易在天上三爻足以岂臣受命应当知也' means 'the Taoist priest said that the *I Ching* in heaven is sufficient with three lines, as I am destined to know this.'
  • 吕蒙入吴王劝其学乃博览羣籍以易爲宗常在孙防座酣醉忽于眠中诵易一部俄而惊起众人皆问之蒙云向梦见伏羲文王周公与我言论世祚兴亡之事日月广明之道莫不穷精极妙未该防政空诵其文耳皆云吕蒙呓语通周易 - A narrative of a dream and its interpretation. '入吴王劝其学' means 'when he entered Wu, the king encouraged him to study,' '乃博览羣籍' means 'so he extensively read various books,' '以易爲宗' means 'with the *I Ching* as the core,' '常在孙防座酣醉' means 'he often drank heavily at Sun's table,' '忽于眠中诵易一部' means 'suddenly, while sleeping, he recited the entire *I Ching*,' '俄而惊起' means 'soon he woke up in shock,' '众人皆问之' means 'everyone asked him,' '蒙云向梦见伏羲文王周公与我言论世祚兴亡之事日月广明之道莫不穷精极妙未该防政空诵其文耳' means 'Lü Meng said that he had dreamed of Fuxi, Wen Wang, and Zhou Gong discussing the rise and fall of dynasties and the broad and bright ways of the sun and moon, nothing was not thoroughly and wonderfully discussed, but it did not encompass the profound mysteries, so he merely recited the text,' '皆云吕蒙呓语通周易' means 'everyone said that Lü Meng was talking in his sleep but understood the *I Ching*.'
  • 洛阳王穆起兵酒泉西伐索嘏长史郭瑀谏不从夜梦乘青龙上天至屋而止觉叹曰屋字尸至也龙飞屋上尸至吾其死矣后果验 - A narrative of a dream and its fulfillment. '起兵酒泉西伐索嘏' means 'rose up in arms in Jiuquan to attack Suo Gu,' '长史郭瑀谏不从' means 'the chief clerk Guo Yu advised against it but was not heeded,' '夜梦乘青龙上天至屋而止' means 'at night, he dreamed of riding a blue dragon up to heaven and stopping at the house,' '觉叹曰屋字尸至也龙飞屋上尸至吾其死矣' means 'upon waking, he sighed, saying that the character for 'house' contains 'corpse,' and the dragon flying to the house means the corpse arrives, so I will die,' '后果验' means 'as it turned out, this was true.'

  • 易 - The *I Ching* or *Book of Changes*, an ancient Chinese divination text and philosophical work.
  • 青龙 - A mythical creature in Chinese mythology, often associated with the east and the element of wood.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This long sentence must be read as a single narrative, with '既觉得之' marking the transition from the dream to the waking realization.
  • This sentence is a continuous narrative, with '觉思之' and '复梦其人来拜谢' marking the transitions between waking thought and subsequent dreams.
  • This sentence is a complex narrative, with '上奏曰' introducing the report and '道士言' introducing the Taoist priest's statement.
  • This sentence is a long narrative, with '俄而惊起' and '蒙云' marking the transitions between the dream and the waking explanation.
  • This sentence is a continuous narrative, with '觉叹曰' and '后果验' marking the transitions between the dream and the fulfillment of the dream.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 緑色犬甚長 - Literal meaning 'a green dog very long,' likely a dream symbol or omen; the color 'green' (緑色) may have specific cultural or symbolic meanings in the context of dreams.
  • 咋天锡 - 'Za' (咋) can mean 'to bite' or 'to attack,' and '天锡' refers to the person Zhang Tianxi; the phrase likely means 'the dog is about to bite Zhang Tianxi.'
  • 从南来 - Means 'coming from the south,' indicating the direction of the dog's movement in the dream.
  • 上牀避之 - '上牀' means 'to get on the bed,' and '避之' means 'to avoid it'; the phrase describes Zhang Tianxi's reaction to the dog.
  • 堕地 - Means 'fall to the ground,' describing the outcome of Zhang Tianxi's attempt to avoid the dog.
  • 緑地锦袍 - '緑地' means 'green ground,' and '锦袍' means 'brocade robe'; this likely refers to a colorful, ornate robe worn by the person.
  • 从南来攻入门 - '从南来' means 'coming from the south,' and '攻入门' means 'to attack and enter the gate'; this describes the military action.
  • 大破之 - Means 'to defeat them decisively.'
  • 鬂眉皓白 - '鬂眉' means 'brows and beard,' and '皓白' means 'white'; this describes the appearance of the person in the dream.
  • 自称子瑜 - '自称' means 'to call oneself,' and '子瑜' is a name; the person in the dream introduces himself as 'Zi Yu.'
  • 地上之事付汝 - '地上之事' means 'earthly matters,' and '付汝' means 'entrusted to you'; the phrase indicates that earthly matters are being entrusted to Zhang Jun.
  • 地下之事付我 - '地下之事' means 'underworld matters,' and '付我' means 'entrusted to me'; the phrase indicates that underworld matters are being entrusted to the person in the dream.
  • 涕泗嘱其子奋 - '涕泗' means 'tears,' and '嘱其子奋' means 'to entrust his son Feng'; the phrase describes the emotional state of the deceased person.
  • 通梦之言而有征 - '通梦之言' means 'the message of the dream,' and '有征' means 'has proof'; the phrase indicates that the dream message is proven true.
  • 内中有一人着衣桶一手把两杖极打之 - '内中' means 'inside,' '着衣桶' means 'wearing a bucket-like garment,' '一手把两杖' means 'holding two sticks with one hand,' and '极打之' means 'beating it fiercely'; the phrase describes a person inside a garment being beaten with sticks.
  • 过三日过三家皆得肉食矣 - '过三日' means 'after three days,' '过三家' means 'passing three households,' and '皆得肉食矣' means 'all get meat to eat'; the phrase describes the fulfillment of the dream message.
  • 满城出菜 - '满城' means 'the entire city,' and '出菜' means 'growing vegetables'; the phrase describes a dream where the entire city is growing vegetables.
  • 地东南倾 - '地东南倾' means 'the ground tilting southeast'; the phrase describes a dream where the ground is tilting.
  • 菜多难为酱也 - '菜多' means 'many vegetables,' and '难为酱也' means 'difficult to make into sauce'; the phrase suggests that having many vegetables makes it difficult to make sauce.
  • 东南倾江左不得平也 - '东南倾' means 'tilting southeast,' and '江左不得平也' means 'the east of the river cannot be peaceful'; the phrase suggests that the tilting ground symbolizes the inability to achieve peace in the east of the river.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 穆之又梦有人称刘鎭军相迎 - Elliptical and grammatically compact; a more complete version would be: 'Mùzhī again dreamed of someone calling himself Lǐu Zhènjūn to come meet him.'
  • 旦占之曰 - Elliptical but means that Mùzhī interpreted the dream and made a statement about it.
  • 吾死矣 - Can be read as either 'I am dead' or 'I will die,' depending on the context.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 劉沙門居彭城病亡妻貧兒幼 - Describes a monk named Liu who lived in Pengcheng, had a poor wife and young children, and died.
  • 遭暴風雨牆宇破壊 - Describes a sudden storm that destroyed the house.
  • 其妻泣擁穉子曰汝爺在豈至於此 - The wife weeps and holds her young child, saying, 'Your father is no longer here, how could this have happened?'
  • 其夜夢沙門將數十人料理宅舍 - That night, she dreams of the monk bringing dozens of people to repair the house.
  • 明日完矣 - The next day, the house was completely repaired.
  • 諸仲務一女顯姨嫁為米元宗妻 - Describes a woman named Zhub Zongwu's daughter, Xianyi, who married Mi Yuanzong.
  • 産亡於家 - She died in childbirth.
  • 俗聞産亡者以墨防靣 - It is a custom to blacken the face of a woman who dies in childbirth.
  • 其母不忍仲務宻自防之無人見者 - Her mother could not bear it, so Zhub Zongwu secretly blackened her face without anyone seeing.
  • 元宗為始新縣丞夢妻來上牀分明見新白妝靣上有墨防 - Mi Yuanzong, as the county magistrate of Shixin, dreamed that his wife came to his bed, clearly seeing her new white makeup with black marks on her face.
  • 有孫氏求官夢雙鳳集其兩拳 - A man named Sun dreamed of two phoenixes gathering on his fists.
  • 果如其所言 - Later, as predicted, it came true.
  • 桓誓字明期居豫章時梅元龍為太守 - A man named Huanshi, whose courtesy name was Mingqi, lived in Yuanchang when Mei Yuanlong was the governor.
  • 先已病矣誓徃看之語元龍云吾昨夜忽夢見君着防衣來迎我 - Mei Yuanlong was already sick, so Huanshi went to see him and said, 'Last night I suddenly dreamed that you were wearing a mourning robe coming to meet me.'
  • 經數日復夢如前云二十八日當拜二十七日桓忽中惡就元龍索麝香丸聞令作凶具二十七日桓亡二十八日龍卒 - After several days, he dreamed the same thing again, saying that on the 28th he would be appointed, but on the 27th, Huanshi suddenly fell ill and went to Mei Yuanlong to get musk pills, hearing that he was preparing funeral items. On the 27th, Huanshi died, and on the 28th, Mei Yuanlong also died.
  • 巴西張尋夢庭生一竹節相似都爲一門 - A man named Zhang Xun from Bazhi dreamed that a bamboo plant grew in his courtyard, with each joint being a door.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '尝梦落殿有孝子扶上他日以问高力士力士云孝子素衣此是韦见素耳帝深然之数日自吏部侍郎拜相' is a complex sequence of events and actions. It describes a dream, a question, a response, and a promotion. The structure can be broken down into: '尝梦落殿' - 'Once dreamed of falling from the palace hall.' '有孝子扶上' - 'Had a filial son to help up.' '他日以问高力士' - 'On another day, asked Gao Lishi.' '力士云孝子素衣此是韦见素耳' - 'Gao Lishi said, 'The filial son in plain clothes is Wei Jiandu.' '帝深然之' - 'The emperor deeply agreed with it.' '数日自吏部侍郎拜相' - 'Several days later, he was promoted from the position of Vice Minister of Personnel to Chancellor, describing a rapid rise in rank.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '未掌纶诰前家有老婢好言梦后多有应' is elliptical and can be interpreted as 'Before taking charge of imperial edicts, there was an old maid in the family who often talked about dreams, and later they often came true.'
  • The sentence '李公乆望除官因访于婢' is elliptical and can be interpreted as 'Mr. Li had long hoped to be appointed to an official position, so he consulted the maid.'
  • The sentence '一日婢至惨然公问故曰昨夜与郎君作梦不好意不欲説' is elliptical and can be interpreted as 'One day, the maid came with a sad expression, and Mr. Li asked her the reason. She said, 'Last night, I had a bad dream with you, and I do not want to tell you.''
  • The sentence '梦有人舁一棺至堂后云且置在此不乆即移入堂中此梦恐非佳也' is elliptical and can be interpreted as 'In the dream, someone carried a coffin to the back of the hall and said, 'Leave it here for now; soon it will be moved into the main hall.' I fear this dream is not good.'
  • The sentence '除中书舍人后知贡举未毕而入相' is elliptical and can be interpreted as 'After being appointed as a Secretary of the Central Secretariat, he was in charge of the imperial examination and then entered the cabinet.'
  • The sentence '王播少贫贱居扬州无人知识唯一军将常接引供给无不罄尽' is elliptical and can be interpreted as 'Wang Bo was poor and lowly when he lived in Yangzhou, and no one knew him except a military officer who often helped and provided for him without any reluctance.'
  • The sentence '杜仆射亚在淮南端午日盛为竞渡之戏诸州征伎乐两县争胜防楼看棚照耀江水数十年未之有也' is elliptical and can be interpreted as 'Duke Du Ya in Huainan held a grand dragon boat race on the Dragon Boat Festival, with performers from various states and two counties competing, and the viewing stands and platforms illuminated the river, a sight not seen in decades.'
  • The sentence '凡扬州之客无贤不肖尽得预焉唯王公不招惆怅自责' is elliptical and can be interpreted as 'All guests in Yangzhou, regardless of their status, were invited to participate, except for Mr. Wang, who felt sad and blamed himself.'
  • The sentence '宗人军将曰某有棚子弟悉在八郎但于棚内看却胜居盘筵间也' is elliptical and can be interpreted as 'The relative and military officer said, 'I have children performing in the viewing stands, all in the eighth brother's group. You can just watch from there, and it will be better than sitting at the banquet.'
  • The sentence '王公曰唯遂往棚时夏初日方照宗人令送法酒一榼曰此甚好适令求得' is elliptical and can be interpreted as 'Mr. Wang said, 'Yes,' and went to the viewing stand. It was early summer, and the sun was shining. The relative ordered a special wine to be sent, saying, 'This is very good, and I just ordered it.'
